failure_surface: Formula cells in column M contain written formula strings (=SUM(H:L)) but do not evaluate to calculated numerical values in the saved workbook, leaving totals uncomputed.
root_cause: The agent used openpyxl to write formula strings directly to cells without triggering recalculation. Openpyxl preserves formulas as static text; when saving, Excel files do not auto-recompute formulas unless recalculation is explicitly forced or the workbook is opened in Excel after writing.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: After writing formula strings to cells, either call wb.calculate() if using a recalculation-capable library setting, compute the expected sum value beforehand and write it as a numeric fallback, or explicitly trigger formula recalculation before saving.
evidence: Code saves: wb.save(output_path) immediately after writing formula strings
No explicit formula recalculation step exists in the workflow between writing formulas and saving
Output shows 'Processed 2 staff members' but verification of calculated totals is missing

memory_items.title: Formula Calculation vs Formula Writing
memory_items.lesson: Writing a formula string via openpyxl does not automatically evaluate it in the saved workbook. Formulas require explicit recalculation or pre-computation to ensure visible calculated values.
memory_items.generalization: When creating new formulas in spreadsheet automation, always verify that the formula either gets evaluated by calling appropriate recalculation methods, or write pre-calculated values alongside formula references.

memory_items.title: Workbook Recalculation Before Save
memory_items.lesson: Excel workbooks saved via Python libraries retain formula strings but may display old cached values or no computation until manually refreshed in Excel.
memory_items.generalization: Always trigger recalculation (via wb.calculation = 'auto' or similar mechanisms) before closing/saving workbooks that contain newly written formulas to ensure accurate displayed values.

memory_items.title: Formula Verification Step
memory_items.lesson: After writing formulas programmatically, spot-check that cells display computed values rather than formula text before considering the task complete.
memory_items.generalization: Include a post-write validation step that reads back formula-containing cells and confirms they either evaluate correctly or contain expected numeric results.

edits.content: **Formulas Require Explicit Recalculation:** When writing formula strings via openpyxl, Excel workbooks save them as static text. To ensure calculated values appear in the saved file, trigger recalculation (e.g., wb.calculation.setCalculationState('recalculate')) before saving, or pre-compute expected values as numeric backups. Always verify formula cells display computed values, not unevaluated formula strings, before finalizing output.
